figures.caption: Figure 1. Task-wise average AUC for novel activity detection in the mid-sequence setting. ER (All), which uses all modalities, closely tracks the RGB-only baseline, indicating that its main logits are largely dominated by RGB. In contrast, MAND maintains consistently higher performance by better exploiting modality-specific evidence.

Table 1. Novel activity detection results on UESTC-MMEA-CL across all combinations of CL methods and novelty scoring functions under three class-incremental settings. Higher is better for AUCT (↑), and lower is better for FPR95T (↓). Results are reported as mean ± std over 5 runs. ▼/△ indicates statistically worse/better performance than the proposed method according to a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p < 0.05).
Figure 3. Task-wise novel activity detection performance. Average AUC over the task stream for the best-performing method pair in each class-incremental setting.

Table 2. Known-class classification results on UESTC-MMEA-CL under three class-incremental settings. Higher is better for ACCT (↑), and lower is better for FGTT (↓). Results are reported as mean ± std over 5 runs. ▼/△ indicates statistically worse/better performance than the proposed method according to a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p < 0.05).
Figure 4. Task-wise known-class classification performance. Average accuracy over the task stream for continual learning methods under each class-incremental setting.

Figure 2. Overview of our modality-aware framework for MMEA OWCL. Left (MoAS, inference): per-sample modality reliabilities are estimated from normalized energy scores and used to adaptively integrate weighted modality logits with the main logits, improving separability between known and novel activities. Right (MoRST, training): modality-specific heads and replay-based modality-logit distillation preserve per-modality decision boundaries across tasks. MoRST stabilizes modality-specific evidence over time, which MoAS then leverages to produce better-calibrated novelty scores.
Figure 5. Effect of different scoring strategies on novelty score separability for Task 2 in the short-sequence (8-class incremental) setting.
